does: This paper articulates an organizational and methodological approach to preparing for catastrophic risks posed by highly-capable AI systems. The authors describe the establishment of a dedicated Preparedness team and the launch of a challenge designed to identify and mitigate frontier risks. The work outlines concrete frameworks for thinking about catastrophic AI scenarios, assessment methodologies for evaluating system behavior under extreme conditions, and structured processes for red-teaming and vulnerability identification before deployment of advanced models.
connects: This work builds on decades of safety research in AI and other high-stakes domains, drawing from dual-use technology governance, biosecurity preparedness, and nuclear safety protocols. It extends recent work on AI alignment, interpretability, and adversarial robustness by applying these technical insights to large-scale system deployment. The paper responds to growing concerns about coordination failures and inadequate safety infrastructure as AI capabilities accelerate, building on earlier calls for proactive safety measures and institutional safeguards from the AI safety research community.
enables: This research enables more systematic identification of catastrophic failure modes before they occur in deployed systems, supporting safer scaling of AI capabilities. It establishes foundations for developing better evaluation metrics and stress-testing protocols applicable across different AI architectures and capabilities. The work points toward strengthened safety standards, institutional practices, and governance frameworks that could prevent catastrophic outcomes while maintaining beneficial AI development, and encourages broader adoption of preparedness mindsets across AI organizations and regulators.
